-# Visual Studio Code - Open Source (""Code - OSS"")

-This repository (""`Code - OSS`"") is where we (Microsoft) develop the [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com) product together with the community. Not only do we work on code and issues here, but we also publish our [roadmap](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Roadmap), [monthly iteration plans](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Iteration-Plans), and our [endgame plans](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Running-the-Endgame). This source code is available to everyone under the standard [MIT license](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/LICENSE.txt).

-[Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com) is a distribution of the `Code - OSS` repository with Microsoft-specific customizations released under a traditional [Microsoft product license](https://code.visualstudio.com/License/).

-[Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com) combines the simplicity of a code editor with what developers need for their core edit-build-debug cycle. It provides comprehensive code editing, navigation, and understanding support along with lightweight debugging, a rich extensibility model, and lightweight integration with existing tools.

-Visual Studio Code is updated monthly with new features and bug fixes. You can download it for Windows, macOS, and Linux on [Visual Studio Code's website](https://code.visualstudio.com/Download). To get the latest releases every day, install the [Insiders build](https://code.visualstudio.com/insiders).
-There are many ways in which you can participate in this project, for example:
-* [Submit bugs and feature requests](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues), and help us verify as they are checked in
-* Review the [documentation](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-docs) and make pull requests for anything from typos to new content.
-If you are interested in fixing issues and contributing directly to the code base,
-please see the document [How to Contribute](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/How-to-Contribute), which covers the following:
-* [The development workflow, including debugging and running tests](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/How-to-Contribute#debugging)
-* Connect with the extension author community on [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-discussions/discussions) or [Slack](https://aka.ms/vscode-dev-community)
-* Follow [@code](https://x.com/code) and let us know what you think!
-See our [wiki](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Feedback-Channels) for a description of each of these channels and information on some other available community-driven channels.

-Many of the core components and extensions to VS Code live in their own repositories on GitHub. For example, the [node debug adapter](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-node-debug) and the [mono debug adapter](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-mono-debug) repositories are separate from each other. For a complete list, please visit the [Related Projects](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki/Related-Projects) page on our [wiki](https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/wiki).

-VS Code includes a set of built-in extensions located in the [extensions](extensions) folder, including grammars and snippets for many languages. Extensions that provide rich language support (inline suggestions, Go to Definition) for a language have the suffix `language-features`. For example, the `json` extension provides coloring for `JSON` and the `json-language-features` extension provides rich language support for `JSON`.

+- The model is disposed when the editor input is disposed.
+- `CustomizationLinkViewItem` and `AICustomizationShortcutsWidget` receive the model instance explicitly.
+- `SessionsView` obtains the model through the contribution helper path, not through a module-global singleton.
+- Do not keep `IAICustomizationItemsModel` as a global singleton.

chat-perf: warm up before measuring TTFT; use steady-state residual for leak check
The chat-perf workflow flaked daily. Two harness issues:
- timeToFirstToken measured the first request in a freshly-launched window,
didCollectInstructions) is bimodal (~250ms warm vs ~900ms cold). This made
TTFT bimodal and flagged random scenarios each run. Fix: send a throwaway
warm-up request and reset to a clean session before measuring, and capture
the before-state after warm-up so its cost is excluded from measured deltas.
- The leak check verdict used cumulative (final - baseline) residual, which
includes one-time first-iteration warm-up (~10MB, ~= threshold). Fix: base
the verdict on steady-state residual measured from the end of iteration 1,
chat-perf: harden npm ci with retry + per-attempt timeout
The setup build job repeatedly hung at 'npm ci' (zero output for ~28min) until
the 30min job timeout cancelled it, blocking the whole workflow. Other workflows
survive this: pr-node-modules.yml wraps npm ci in a 5-attempt retry loop. Mirror
that here, and additionally wrap each attempt in 'timeout 900' so a hung install
is killed and retried rather than consuming the entire job timeout. Bump the
setup job timeout-minutes 30 -> 50 to leave headroom for retries.
chat-perf: skip electron/playwright binary downloads during npm ci to avoid postinstall hang
chat-perf: fix TTFT bimodality — drop v8.gc_stats trace category; revert warm-up
Measured root cause (via trace analysis of slow vs fast runs): every slow run's
timeToFirstToken is a single ~550ms MajorGC that lands in the measured window,
of which ~97% is V8.GC_OBJECT_DUMP_STATISTICS. That phase only runs when the
'disabled-by-default-v8.gc_stats' trace category is enabled — which the harness
enabled — inflating a normal ~15ms GC to ~550ms. Whether such a GC lands in the
request window is probabilistic, producing the bimodal ~250ms/~900ms TTFT. It was
a measurement artifact, not a product regression.
Fix: drop the gc_stats trace category (keep v8.gc + disabled-by-default-v8.gc,
which still emit the MajorGC/MarkCompactor events we count). Also revert the
earlier warm-up change, which was based on a wrong (cold-instruction-index)
hypothesis and introduced layout-count asymmetry between baseline and test.
chat-perf: add opt-in --gc-object-stats flag; document baseline is optional
Preserve the V8 gc_stats deep-dive capability behind an explicit --gc-object-stats
flag (off by default) so it's available for deliberate GC investigations but never
corrupts timing benchmarks. Also clarify in the skill that --no-baseline runs are

first-class: a baseline is only for comparison, not required to just measure a build.","LLM_ERROR: Error code: 402 - {'error': {'message': 'The account associated with the API Key is in arrears and the accessed service ID has been isolated. Fix bugs related to setting the right Chat model.
This patch was originally created to address a bug where a Try Again in
the Chat window would not allow a different model to be selected.
Multiple inconsistencies were found related to feeding the model
selection widget's current state in various places. This patch
centralizes this in getSelectedModelRequestOptions() to avoid needing to
keep multiple call sites in sync going forward.
More specifically, several resend and confirmation paths passed only the
selected model ID. That can drop editor-scoped model configuration, such
as custom per-model settings, and cause follow-up requests to use
configuration that does not match the visible model picker state. The
new helper keeps the selected model ID and its scoped configuration
together whenever the widget builds Chat request options.

Skip CG NOTICE steps on CI test builds
Pull/Apply CG NOTICE only matter when a build packages a notice it will
ship. CI test jobs (VSCODE_CIBUILD=true: the Electron/Browser/Remote Tests
jobs) compile and build a throwaway product to run tests against, then
discard it -- they never publish a notice. Running the CG NOTICE generate +
apply there is wasted work, and it is the sole source of the intermittent
'Apply CG NOTICE' failures (the detached deemon daemon getting killed during
the long test phase, so 'deemon --attach' exits non-zero).
Gate both notice steps behind 'ne(VSCODE_CIBUILD, true)', matching the
existing guard already used for the other publish-only steps in these
templates (Wait for CLI artifact, Group Policy, Explorer dll, appx). Both
the background detach and the attach are gated by the same condition so they

stay paired. Publishing builds (CIBUILD=false) are unaffected.","LLM_ERROR: Error code: 402 - {'error': {'message': 'The account associated with the API Key is in arrears and the accessed service ID has been isolated. Fix intermittent Apply CG NOTICE failures
The Pull/Apply CG NOTICE steps overwrite ThirdPartyNotices.txt with the
CG-generated notice before gulp packages it. They run downloadNotice.ts via
deemon: Pull detaches a background daemon, Apply attaches to collect it.
downloadNotice.ts is non-fatal by design (always exits 0), but the
'deemon --attach' wrapper exits non-zero when the daemon was killed during
the long compile/test phase, hard-failing the job. This only bites CI test
jobs (VSCODE_CIBUILD=true), which build a throwaway product and never ship a

[inductor] Fix bool minimum/maximum crash in index propagation

`torch.minimum`/`torch.maximum` on boolean tensors crash under `torch.compile(backend=""inductor"")` with `ValueError: The argument 'False' is not comparable.`, while eager mode works. Reported in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/188230 (repro: threshold -> torch.eq -> torch.empty_like -> torch.minimum). The root cause is that inductor feeds boolean sympy values into `Min`/`Max`, which sympy rejects because booleans are not orderable. Two independent call sites are involved: (1) index propagation builds a symbolic `Min`/`Max` over the operand expressions, and (2) value-range/bounds analysis calls `sympy.Min`/`sympy.Max` on boolean ranges. The second site stays masked behind the first until the first is fixed.
Guards both call sites against boolean operands, mirroring the existing pattern in each file:

- `torch/_inductor/index_propagation.py`: `SymPyOps.minimum`/`maximum` return `NotImplemented` when the promoted result type is `torch.bool`, so index propagation falls back to the regular ops handler (same convention as the existing `floordiv`/`mod`/`remainder` guards).

- `torch/utils/_sympy/value_ranges.py`: `SymPyValueRangeAnalysis.minimum`/`maximum` delegate to `and_`/`or_` when either operand `is_bool` (boolean min == logical-and, max == logical-or), matching the existing `bitwise_and`/`bitwise_or` handling.
Adds regression tests covering both paths.
-> Pass 231. Fail 0. Skip 1 (Z3 not installed).  (incl. new test_bool_minimum_maximum)
(test_sympy_utils.py has no standing fbcode target; runs in PyTorch OSS CI via `python test/...`)
arc lint on all 4 changed files -> ok No lint issues.
Without the fix, the new inductor test fails with the exact `ValueError: The argument 'False' is not comparable.`

Raise a clear error when linalg.eigh/svd workspace exceeds 32-bit LAPACK limit
Root cause: the CPU LAPACK path indexes its workspace with a 32-bit int. LAPACK
returns the optimal workspace size from its query call as a floating point value,
which lapack_work_to_int() rounds and casts to int. For a large matrix the
required workspace can exceed INT_MAX -- for syevd with eigenvectors it is
approximately 2 * n**2, so it overflows once n is in the low tens of thousands
(e.g. torch.linalg.eigh on a 33000 x 33000 matrix). The cast then wrapped to a
bogus, often negative, size and the subsequent workspace allocation and LAPACK
call failed with an opaque error instead of telling the user what went wrong.
Fix: lapack_work_to_int() is the single choke point every CPU LAPACK routine
funnels its workspace size through (eigh, svd, qr, lstsq, ...), so the guard is
added there. When the queried size exceeds INT_MAX it now raises a
TORCH_CHECK explaining that the matrix is too large for the 32-bit LAPACK
interface and suggesting the input be processed in chunks or PyTorch be built
against a 64-bit-integer (ILP64) LAPACK, instead of overflowing.
The overflow is specific to the CPU LAPACK backend; the CUDA path uses cuSOLVER
and is unaffected, so the regression test is CPU-only and gated on available
# new regression test (allocates a ~4.4GB input, skips when RAM is low)
# existing coverage, unchanged behavior on normal sizes
Manually confirmed torch.linalg.eigh on a 33000 x 33000 float32 matrix now
raises ""The matrix is too large for the current LAPACK backend ..."" with the
required and maximum workspace sizes, and that eigh/eigvalsh/svd/qr remain

numerically correct on ordinary sizes for float32/float64/complex64/complex128.","LLM_ERROR: Error code: 402 - {'error': {'message': 'The account associated with the API Key is in arrears and the accessed service ID has been isolated. Fix type confusion in Stream.__eq__/__ne__ against non-Stream objects
Root cause: THPStream_richcompare only special-cased None and otherwise
reinterpret_cast<THPStream*>-ed the other operand unconditionally, then read
stream_id/device_index/device_type off it. When the other operand was any
non-Stream Python object (e.g. `stream == 5`), those reads landed at Stream
field offsets inside an unrelated object, returning garbage and potentially
Fix: gate the cast on THPStream_Check(other). A Stream compared with any
non-Stream object (including None) is now unequal for Py_EQ/Py_NE without
touching the operand's memory, and ordering comparisons return
Py_NotImplemented so Python raises TypeError instead of silently yielding
False. This also fixes `stream != None` previously returning False.

Fix torch.pow docstring: scalar params are Number, not float
The torch.pow docstring typed the scalar base (self) and the scalar
exponent as float, but the ATen schemas pow.Scalar(Scalar self, Tensor
exponent) and pow.Tensor_Scalar(Tensor self, Scalar exponent) accept any
Scalar (Number). The docstring's own examples pass integers
(torch.pow(a, 2) and base = 2). Use Number, matching the house style used

by arange, clamp, and the other scalar parameters in this file.","LLM_ERROR: Error code: 402 - {'error': {'message': 'The account associated with the API Key is in arrears and the accessed service ID has been isolated. Add a `capture_error_mode` parameter to `torch.cuda.make_graphed_callables` (default `""global""`, preserving current behavior), forwarded to the two internal `torch.cuda.graph` forward/backward capture contexts. This lets callers select `""thread_local""` so that unsafe CUDA operations on *other* threads (e.g. an async dataloader's background host-to-device copies) do not invalidate the capture; the default `""global""` mode raises on any thread's unsafe CUDA op during capture. Mirrored to `xplat/caffe2`.
release notes: Added the ability to pass capture_error_mode to torch.cuda.make_graphed_callables.

Fix test name detection for Git-style paths

`python_test_file_to_test_name()` used `os.path.sep` to recognize files under `test/`. On Windows, that makes the helper look for `test\`, while Git and GitHub changed-file paths use `/`, so directly changed test files can be missed by target-determination heuristics.
Normalize backslashes to `/` before matching the repo-relative `test/` prefix, and add a focused unit test covering Git-style paths, Windows-style paths, non-test paths, and non-Python files.
`spin fixlint` and `spin quicklint` were attempted but blocked locally during lintrunner setup on Windows because the `CLANGFORMAT` initializer reports `Unsupported platform: Windows/Windows-AMD64`.

Enable multi-arch official CUDA docker images

Root cause: the release matrix only emitted linux/amd64 for CUDA entries, and Dockerfile install logic forced linux/arm64 builds to use CPU wheels. That produced x86-only CUDA image tags and prevented publishing arm64 variants under the same tags.

Fix: emit linux/amd64,linux/arm64 platforms for CUDA runtime/devel release matrix entries, update Dockerfile to choose CPU vs CUDA wheel indexes by CUDA_PATH instead of TARGETPLATFORM, and remove the arm64 exclusion when installing gcc in CUDA images. Also update nightly tag push condition to run for CUDA runtime images independent of explicit platform string.

[MPS] Add solo large-tensor allocator to MPSHeapAllocatorImpl
Adds an opt-in ""solo"" allocation mode for tensors at or above a
configurable size threshold (default kMinLargeAlloc = 10 MB). Instead of
sub-allocating from a shared MTLHeap, each large tensor gets its own
MTLBuffer via newBufferWithLength. Freed buffers are held in a per-size
free list (m_solo_cache) for same-size reuse across steps; emptyCache()
Motivation: PyTorch's existing large-tensor path allocates from a shared
1 GB MTLHeap that can only be released when all its buffers are
simultaneously free -- which never occurs during ML training. The solo
path gives each large tensor independent lifetime, eliminating this
accumulation and matching the behavior of CUDA's per-tensor cudaMalloc.
In practice this keeps peak driver-allocated memory within ~200 MB of
live tensor memory across multi-thousand-step training runs, vs 5-8x
added to MPSHeapAllocatorImpl; heap path is unchanged and default.
- alloc_buffer_block(): solo fast-path bypasses heap; checks per-size
- free(): solo buffers (heap == nullptr) return to m_solo_cache instead
- emptyCache(): drains m_solo_cache after GPU sync; uses explicit
large_alloc_threshold:<MB> key overrides the default threshold.
Exposes the solo allocator toggle through the standard MPS hooks layer:
- IMPSHooks: add virtual setAllocatorSettings(const std::string&) with
empty default so non-MPS builds are unaffected.
[MPS] Test solo allocator opt-in and pool behavior
- Solo mode opt-in and revert via set_allocator_settings()
- Driver vs allocated memory stays bounded in solo mode (no heap
- Per-size buffer reuse: same-size tensors recycle from m_solo_cache
- emptyCache() drains the free list to zero driver memory

[MPS] Reject complex logaddexp2 inputs before Metal dispatch

logaddexp2 on MPS is only implemented for real floating dtypes, but complex inputs currently reach the generic Metal binary kernel path. ComplexFloat is then interpreted as a float2 kernel specialization and fails while creating the Metal function state, producing an opaque internal error instead of the same unsupported-dtype failure users see on CPU.
Add an MPS dispatch guard for logaddexp2 that rejects complex common dtypes before launching the Metal kernel, and add a regression test for broadcast ComplexFloat inputs.
